verify.plainSummary: The hackathon is real and every detail in the claim checks out against its official page. It is "Stellar Hacks: Real-World ZK," run by the Stellar Development Foundation (the nonprofit behind the Stellar blockchain) on the DoraHacks platform. Submissions close 29 June 2026 at 19:00 UTC (7pm UTC), the prize pool is 10,000 US dollars (paid in Stellar's XLM token, split across five places), and to qualify a project must have a public open-source code repository with a clear README, a 2-3 minute demo video, and must genuinely use zero-knowledge cryptography (a privacy/math technique that proves something is true without revealing the underlying data) in a way that connects to Stellar, such as verifying a proof inside a Stellar smart contract.
verify.corrections: None. All five checked elements match. One nuance worth knowing: the page's human-readable "Key Dates" text labels the deadline "June 29, 12:00PM PST," and the header countdown widget rendered "2026/06/29 20:00" (because my browser was in a UTC+1 timezone). Both are slightly confusing surface labels, but the page's own machine-readable application data gives the canonical deadline as endTime=1782759600000 ms = 2026-06-29T19:00:00Z, which exactly matches the claim's "19:00 UTC." Likewise startTime=1781506800000 ms = 2026-06-15T07:00:00Z. The $10,000 pool is denominated/paid in XLM and breaks down as 1st $5,000 / 2nd $2,000 / 3rd $1,250 / 4th $1,000 / 5th $750 — the claim's "~$10,000 USD" total is accurate.

citations.source: Live DoraHacks hackathon page (rendered in a real browser via Playwright; the site is behind an AWS WAF CAPTCHA so curl/WebFetch return 405/CAPTCHA — the browser passed the challenge)
citations.proves: Page title and H1 = 'Stellar Hacks: Real-World ZK'; sidebar 'Prize pool 10,000 USD'; organizer link 'Stellar Development Foundation' (/org/3096/hackathon); Submission Requirements section lists 'An open-source repo' (public GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket + clear README.md), 'A short demo video' (2-3 minute walkthrough), and 'ZK + Stellar' with text 'The ZK should be load-bearing... verifying proofs in a contract, or otherwise integrating Stellar testnet or mainnet'; prize breakdown 1st $5,000 / 2nd $2,000 / 3rd $1,250 / 4th $1,000 / 5th $750 in XLM.

citations.source: DoraHacks page Nuxt application state (window.__NUXT__.data['hackathon-page-stellar-hacks-zk'].hackathon._data), read via browser_evaluate — the canonical machine-readable timestamps behind the rendered widget
citations.proves: endTime = 1782759600000 ms = 2026-06-29T19:00:00.000Z (confirms deadline exactly = 19:00 UTC, resolving the page's looser 'June 29 12:00PM PST' label); startTime = 1781506800000 ms = 2026-06-15T07:00:00.000Z (submissions open).

citations.source: WebSearch independent index corroboration (separate retrieval path from the local note)
citations.proves: Search index independently returns prize pool 10,000 USD, deadline June 29 2026 19:00 UTC, GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket + demo video required, organized by Stellar Development Foundation.

citations.source: Local pre-existing research brief (secondary, cross-checked against the live source above — included for traceability, not relied on as primary)
citations.proves: Prior brief asserted name, SDF sponsor, $10,000 pool, 2026-06-29 19:00 UTC deadline, 2-3 min video, open-source repo+README, load-bearing ZK touching Stellar — all of which the live primary source independently confirms.

verify.plainSummary: Moonlight Protocol is a real project: a privacy layer for payments built on Stellar (a blockchain) by The Aha Company, with a live GitHub organization holding 17 public code repositories — including the browser-extension wallet, an SDK (developer toolkit), and the smart contracts — that were actively being worked on as recently as June 17-19, 2026. It received roughly $135,000 (134,990 in XLM, Stellar's currency) from the Stellar Community Fund's round 37 grant program, and it is name-checked in Stellar's own official privacy blog. The one nuance: the public code repositories do not currently carry a formal open-source license file, so the code is publicly viewable but not strictly "open-source" in the licensed sense; everything else in the claim checks out.
verify.corrections: The claim is essentially accurate. Two small refinements: (1) "open-source" is only partly right — the repos (browser-wallet, moonlight-sdk, soroban-core) are PUBLIC and readable, but none contains a LICENSE file (GitHub API reports license=null), so they are publicly-available source rather than formally OSI-licensed open source. (2) The funding figure is exact: $134,990 (paid in XLM), which rounds to the claimed ~$135k. Also a naming note: the GitHub org is "Moonlight-Protocol" (capitalized) and the company contact appears as both theaha.co and theahaco.co; the org's own description tags itself "[wip]" (work-in-progress), consistent with the claimed testnet beta rather than a mainnet launch.

citations.proves: Live org exists (id 203846951), 17 public repos, created 2025-03-18, description: '[wip] A privacy protocol built on top of Stellar's soroban smart contract capabilities'
browser-wallet, moonlight-sdk, and soroban-core all exist as public (private:false) repos; soroban-core described 'Core Contracts', pushed 2026-06-17

Recent June 2026 commits confirmed: moonlight-sdk last commit 2026-06-17 by Gorka Ludlow <gorka@theaha.co> ('docs: how to run unit vs integration tests #37'); soroban-core 2026-06-17, browser-wallet 2026-06-17
It is a real browser extension: Manifest V3, name 'Stellar Custom Wallet', background service_worker + popup, gecko (Firefox) settings
Testnet beta confirmed: README references '.env.seed ... pre-filled with Moonlight Beta defaults' and auto-configuring the extension on first load
Working testnet config: rpcUrl 'https://soroban-testnet.stellar.org', testnet network passphrase, testnet contract IDs in integration-guide.md
The Aha Company connection: contracts depend on 'theahaco/rs-soroban-sdk' fork; commit author email is gorka@theaha.co
Real Soroban smart contracts exist (privacy-channel, token, channel-auth) built with soroban-sdk, crate-type cdylib
Stellar's own privacy blog name-checks it: 'Moonlight, for example, is a UTXO-based privacy layer on Stellar funded by the Stellar Community Fund.'
SCF round-37 funding confirmed: Moonlight Protocol received $134,990 worth of XLM in Stellar Community Fund Round 37 for private payments via UTXO model + provider-mediated channels

Live website: 'A privacy layer for blockchain payments... Built on Stellar with Soroban smart contracts. Funded by the Stellar Community Fund.' Copyright The Aha Company, contact moonlight@theahaco.co
Correction evidence: license=null (no formal open-source LICENSE file) despite repo being public; default_branch main, open_issues 3

verify.plainSummary: Moonlight is a Stellar privacy wallet whose privacy is statistical, not cryptographic. It does NOT use zero-knowledge proofs; its own whitepaper explicitly rejects them and there is zero ZK code in its repos. Privacy comes from splitting funds into random-sized chunks (UTXOs) plus a mandatory trusted Privacy Provider that submits the transaction so the user address never appears on-chain. Amounts and the UTXO set are plaintext on-chain, the provider keeps off-chain logs linking identity to activity (custodial compliance), and the beta is seed-phrase only, XLM-only, no USDC.
verify.corrections: None; every sub-claim verified true. Nuance: the relayer hides the address from outside observers but the provider itself logs identity-to-activity. The contract generically supports any single Stellar asset, but only XLM is wired up in the beta, so XLM-only is correct in practice.

citations.proves: Explicitly rejects ZK as heavyweight circuits and trusted-setup, choosing lighter techniques; only ZK mention in any repo.
PROVIDER_THRESHOLD=1; errors ProviderThresholdNotMet without a registered provider Ed25519 signature, so relayer is mandatory.
UtxoMeta stores amount as plain i128, no encryption/commitment; amounts plaintext on-chain.
Amount partitioned into random-sized UTXOs; single UTXO is matchable; provider submits on user behalf to hide address; no ZK.
Provider holds off-chain data linking on-chain activity to SEP-10 user sessions; custodial compliance, no view key.
SEED_ASSET_CODE=XLM; zero USDC and zero passkey/webauthn hits in wallet source; BIP39 mnemonic only.

verify.plainSummary: This is real. NethermindEth/stellar-private-payments is a working reference implementation of "Privacy Pools" (a way to send money privately while still letting an operator block bad actors) built on Stellar. It uses zero-knowledge proofs — math that lets you prove a transaction is valid without revealing the amounts or who is involved — generated in your own browser, and verified by a smart contract on Stellar's free testnet. The team itself labels it work-in-progress and explicitly states it has NOT been security-audited, so it should not be used with real money yet.
verify.corrections: None. Every element checks out. Minor naming note: the GitHub Pages demo brands the running app as "PoolStellar," but it is the same NethermindEth/stellar-private-payments project. The deployed testnet pool moves Stellar's native asset (XLM) — confirmed by deployments.json showing "asset":{"kind":"native"} — not a wrapped token.

citations.proves: States it is a 'reference implementation of privacy pools for Stellar' using Groth16 proofs generated via Circom circuits; lists Browser-Based Proving via WebAssembly, ASP membership + non-membership trees, a Circom Groth16 Verifier on-chain contract, and an explicit WARNING that 'The code has not yet been audited' plus a 'Not Audited' limitation.
Confirms real Circom circuits exist (transaction.circom, policyTransaction.circom, merkleTree.circom, selectiveDisclosure.circom, poseidon2/*, smt/* etc.) defining the ZK constraints.
File header reads 'Groth16 verifier contract for Circom proofs on Soroban'; imports soroban_sdk contract/contractimpl and Groth16Proof/VerificationKeyBytes for BN254 on-chain verification.
Contains asp-membership and asp-non-membership contracts plus the pool contract, confirming ASP membership + non-membership safeguards.

network:'testnet' with real deployed contract IDs for verifier, asp_membership, asp_non_membership, and a pool whose asset is {"kind":"native"} (native XLM), proving deployment on Stellar testnet moving native XLM.
Client-side WebAssembly init (import init from './web.js', initializeWasm) confirming proof generation runs in the browser via WASM.
Live docs/demo site exists, branded PoolStellar, showing 'Network Testnet', pool/ASP membership+non-membership contracts, BN254 keys, and Circuits NOTICE links.

verify.plainSummary: Stellar did add native zero-knowledge cryptography building blocks into its blockchain, exactly as claimed. In the Protocol 25 upgrade (code-named X-Ray, live January 2026), Soroban smart contracts gained built-in functions for the BN254 curve (the math operations g1_add, g1_mul, and pairing_check) and for the Poseidon/Poseidon2 hash functions; these are the same primitives Ethereum exposes via its EIP-196/EIP-197 precompiles, making Stellar compatible with existing ZK tooling. The follow-up Protocol 26 upgrade (Yardstick, live May 2026) added more BN254 helper functions that move heavy math into the network core, making proof checking cheaper. Together these let a contract verify a Groth16/SNARK proof (a compact cryptographic receipt proving a statement is true without revealing the underlying data) on-chain far more cheaply than doing the math in contract code. One nuance: the host functions are low-level building blocks; a contract still needs verifier logic on top, which is exactly what the open-source RISC Zero verifier in the research repos provides.
verify.corrections: Essentially none — every specific assertion checks out. Two small clarifications: (1) CAP-0074's actual function names are bn254_g1_add / bn254_g1_mul / bn254_multi_pairing_check, which the Soroban SDK surfaces to contract authors as g1_add / g1_mul / pairing_check (as used in the real risc0-verifier contract), so the claim's naming is accurate at the developer-facing level. (2) The host functions alone don't verify Groth16 proofs by themselves — they are primitives; a verifier contract built on top of them does. Protocol 26's cheaper verification is delivered via CAP-0080 (additional BN254 host functions: multi-scalar multiplication, scalar-field arithmetic, curve-membership checks), which the claim correctly summarizes as making proof verification cheaper.

citations.proves: Titled Host functions for BN254; adds bn254_g1_add, bn254_g1_mul, bn254_multi_pairing_check; targets protocol version 25 (min_supported_protocol 25); explicitly states it adds support for the precompiles specified in EIP-196 and EIP-197.
Adds Poseidon and Poseidon2 permutation host functions (poseidon_permutation, poseidon2_permutation) as ZK-friendly hash primitives, targeting protocol version 25; Final status, created Oct 8 2025.

States X-Ray (Protocol 25) introduced native host functions for zero-knowledge-friendly primitives (BN254 and Poseidon/Poseidon2); describes g1_add, g1_mul, pairing_check and poseidon/poseidon2 as building blocks for proof verification.

citations.source: Stellar blog Announcing Stellar X-Ray Protocol 25
citations.proves: Protocol 25 is code-named X-Ray and introduces native BN254 and Poseidon primitives giving feature parity with Ethereum EIP-196 and EIP-197 precompiles for efficient on-chain zk-SNARK verification.

Protocol 26 is code-named Yardstick (live mainnet May 6 2026) and adds additional BN254 cryptographic host functions (CAP-0080: multi-scalar multiplication, scalar-field arithmetic, curve-membership checks) that make ZK proof verification meaningfully cheaper.

A real Soroban contract calls env.crypto().bn254() and uses bn.g1_mul, bn.g1_add, and bn.pairing_check to perform the Groth16 pairing check over BN254, demonstrating the host functions verify Groth16/SNARK proofs on-chain.
Documents on-chain RISC Zero Groth16 (BN254) proof verification for Stellar/Soroban, confirming the real-world Groth16 verification use case enabled by these host functions.

verify.plainSummary: A "passkey" is the modern fingerprint/face-unlock login that replaces passwords. The WebAuthn PRF extension is a real, shipped browser feature that lets a website ask the passkey to spit out a secret number tied to that specific passkey plus a chosen "salt" (an extra input string). That number is deterministic — the same passkey and same salt always produce the exact same 32-byte (256-bit) secret — so it can be turned into an encryption key that locks and unlocks a user's private data. Real products (Bitwarden and Dashlane in production, plus wwWallet as an open reference implementation) already use this to encrypt vaults, and it works in current Chrome. Browser extensions can use WebAuthn when host permissions cover the RP ID, but the ceremony should run from an extension page, tab, or side panel because popups can close during the credential prompt.
verify.corrections: The core claim is correct. One precision on the Chrome version: the PRF extension itself originally shipped enabled-by-default around Chrome 116 (2023), not 132. "Chrome ~132+" is accurate as the point where PRF became reliably usable via platform authenticators on macOS (iCloud Keychain), and full PRF-on-create support for Windows Hello only landed later in Chrome 147 — so PRF availability is authenticator-and-OS dependent, not a single clean version cutoff. Also a nuance on the prior art: Bitwarden's own marketing blog uses hypothetical "can use" language, but Bitwarden's contributing docs and help pages confirm PRF-based vault decryption is a shipped, generally-available feature, so it is genuine production prior art. Determinism has the standard caveat that the raw PRF output should be run through HKDF (a key-derivation step) rather than used directly as a key, and the spec hashes the salt with a fixed "WebAuthn PRF" context string before evaluation — neither of which breaks the stable/deterministic property the claim asserts.

citations.source: MDN Web Docs — Web Authentication extensions (prf)
citations.proves: MDN documents the prf extension as a random-oracle PRF that 'will always return the same value for the same input' (deterministic) and explicitly cites generating a symmetric encryption key as a use case, with a credentials.get() prf.eval.first salt example.

citations.source: Yubico Developers — Developer's Guide to PRF
citations.proves: States PRF delivers a deterministic 32-byte high-entropy secret (Input Keying Material) that is the same across authentication ceremonies for the same credential+salt, and should be passed through HKDF to derive purpose-bound encryption keys.

citations.source: Bitwarden contributing docs — Passkeys for decryption (PRF relying-party implementation)
citations.proves: Bitwarden uses the WebAuthn PRF extension in production: a PRF symmetric key derived from the authenticator's internal secret plus a Bitwarden-provided salt is used to encrypt/decrypt the user's vault, working in the web vault and browser extension.

citations.source: Corbado — Passkeys & WebAuthn PRF for End-to-End Encryption (2026)
citations.proves: Confirms Dashlane and Bitwarden as production adopters of PRF for encrypted-vault access, names wwWallet's HKDF keystore as a reference implementation, and documents Chrome version progression (PRF via iCloud Keychain on macOS in Chrome 132, full Windows Hello PRF-on-create enabled by default in Chrome 147).

citations.source: Chromium blink-dev — Intent to Ship: WebAuthn PRF extension
citations.proves: Official Chromium Intent-to-Ship thread (2023) for the WebAuthn PRF extension, establishing it as a real, shipped Chrome feature with Dashlane/1Password vendor support cited.

citations.source: MDN — Use the WebAuthn API in web extensions
citations.proves: Confirms browser extensions (Chrome 122+, Firefox 150+) can call navigator.credentials.get()/create() and assert an RP ID for domains in host_permissions, establishing that WebAuthn (and thus PRF on get()) is invokable from an extension/popup context.

Spec-level explainer confirming PRF is built on the authenticator hmac-secret, that website salts are hashed with a fixed 'WebAuthn PRF' context string before evaluation, yielding a deterministic per-credential output usable for key derivation.

verify.plainSummary: Stellar does let people control blockchain accounts with passkeys — the same fingerprint/face-unlock login (called WebAuthn) you already use on phones and laptops. In June 2024 a network upgrade (Protocol 21) added a built-in function that lets Stellar's smart contracts (Soroban) check passkey signatures, which uses a cryptography curve called secp256r1. On top of that, developer kalepail published an open-source toolkit, passkey-kit, that makes passkey-controlled wallets buildable; he has since marked it "legacy" and points new builders to his successor, smart-account-kit, which wraps audited OpenZeppelin contracts. The capability is real and usable, with the caveat that passkey-kit itself is unaudited and superseded.
verify.corrections: Nothing in the claim is wrong. Two clarifications worth noting: (1) The exact protocol mechanism is CAP-0051 "Smart Contract Host Functionality: Secp256r1 Verification" (status Final), which adds the host function verify_sig_ecdsa_secp256r1, included in Protocol 21 — so "Protocol 21+" is accurate. (2) passkey-kit's own README now flags it as a "legacy precursor" and unaudited, directing new projects to kalepail's smart-account-kit (built on OpenZeppelin stellar-contracts). The claim's statement that it is "buildable" remains true, but anyone acting on it should prefer the successor kit.

citations.proves: CAP-0051 'Smart Contract Host Functionality: Secp256r1 Verification', Status Final, Protocol Version 21, adds host function verify_sig_ecdsa_secp256r1 verifying the secp256r1 curve, described as the standard behind passkeys/WebAuthn.

Open-source TypeScript SDK by kalepail for creating/managing Stellar smart wallets using passkeys; supports Secp256r1 (passkey), Ed25519 and policy signers; README marks it a legacy precursor, unaudited, pointing to smart-account-kit.

Active repo (Apache-2.0) by kalepail: TS SDK for deploying/managing OpenZeppelin Smart Account contracts on Stellar/Soroban with WebAuthn passkey authentication; the recommended successor built on OpenZeppelin stellar-contracts.
Independent confirmation that secp256r1 verification (verify_sig_ecdsa_secp256r1, export name 3 module c) shipped in Protocol 21, enabling passkey-signed Soroban contract transactions.

verify.plainSummary: Circle CCTP supports Stellar with native USDC (burn-and-mint), CCTP V2 with Hooks/CctpForwarder for atomic mint-and-forward in one Soroban invocation, domain id 27, on both mainnet and testnet.
verify.corrections: The bridge claim is correct for mint-and-forward only. It does not imply atomic shield/deposit into the privacy pool.

citations.proves: Stellar domain 27, mainnet+testnet

verify.plainSummary: WXT (wxt.dev) is a genuine, very actively maintained tool for building browser extensions (the small add-ons that live in Chrome/Firefox). All the specific claims check out: it is built on Vite (a popular fast web build tool), supports React + TypeScript, generates its config from files you create ("file-based entrypoints"), has live hot-reload during development, and produces extensions for Chrome, Firefox and others from one setup using the modern "Manifest V3" format. It can run WebAssembly (high-performance compiled code in the browser) — in fact its default security policy already includes the required 'wasm-unsafe-eval' permission. The named alternative, Plasmo, does appear comparatively stale: its last published release was over a year ago (May 2025) versus WXT's May 2026, though Plasmo's repo is not formally archived.
verify.corrections: Nothing in the claim is wrong. One refinement: the claim says WXT "supports bundling WebAssembly (with a wasm-unsafe-eval manifest CSP)" — in reality WXT goes further and ships 'wasm-unsafe-eval' in its DEFAULT MV3 extension-pages content_security_policy, so WASM works out of the box rather than requiring manual CSP configuration. On Plasmo: "maintenance mode" is a fair characterization based on release cadence (latest npm release 2025-05-17, ~13 months stale as of 2026-06), but its GitHub repo is NOT archived and shows recent commit activity, so it is not formally abandoned — "comparatively in maintenance mode" is accurate as a relative statement.

citations.proves: WXT is actively maintained: 10,037 stars, archived=false, pushed_at=2026-06-21 (same day as verification); description '⚡ Next-gen Web Extension Framework'
Latest published version 0.20.26 on 2026-05-11; WXT is current on npm
Plasmo latest version 0.90.5 published 2025-05-17 — ~13 months stale vs WXT, supporting 'comparatively in maintenance mode'
Plasmo repo is NOT archived (archived=false) and pushed_at=2026-06-20, so not formally abandoned — qualifies the 'maintenance mode' framing

Confirms 'File Based Entrypoints' (manifest generated from files), 'Fast Dev Mode' with 'Lightning fast HMR', builds for 'Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari', supports MV3, and 'Works with any front-end framework with a Vite plugin'; TypeScript supported by default
DEFAULT_MV3_EXTENSION_PAGES_CSP = "script-src 'self' 'wasm-unsafe-eval'; object-src 'self';" — WXT ships wasm-unsafe-eval in its default MV3 CSP, proving built-in WASM support
Official React support via '@wxt-dev/module-react' and '@vitejs/plugin-react'; confirms Vite-based architecture ('WXT supports any framework with a Vite plugin')

verify.plainSummary: The claim is accurate. Stellar's own developer documentation states plainly that "Stellar is a public blockchain: every transaction is recorded onchain and visible to anyone" — so you cannot make everything private by default. Privacy only exists inside two specific tools: Privacy Pools (where the money put in and taken out is still publicly visible, but payments between people already inside the pool can be hidden) and Confidential Tokens (which hide amounts and balances but keep the sender and receiver addresses public). The honest way to describe this is "shielded transfers" — Stellar's own materials use the word "shielded" — not "a fully private wallet."
verify.corrections: Essentially none — the claim matches the primary sources. Two minor refinements for precision: (a) The claim's example is Privacy Pools and Confidential Tokens, but the docs also list adjacent ZK building blocks (onchain ZK verifiers, BLS12-381/BN254/Poseidon primitives); these are cryptographic plumbing, not a third "make-everything-private" mode, so they do not weaken the claim. (b) On "arbitrary smart-contract calls cannot be made private": the docs do not explicitly assert this prohibition; rather, they describe privacy as scoped to specific use cases (payments, settlement, payroll, confidential tokens) and never offer general transaction/contract-call privacy. So this sub-point is correct in substance (no general private execution is offered) but is an inference from absence/scope, not a verbatim denial in the docs. (c) On terminology: the developers.stellar.org privacy page itself does not use the phrase "shielded transfers," but Stellar's "Stellar Private Payments" / financial-privacy materials do describe "shielded deposits, transfers, and withdrawals" and individual records remaining "shielded," so the "shielded transfers" framing is genuinely Stellar's own.

citations.source: Stellar official developer docs — Privacy on Stellar
citations.proves: Verbatim: 'Stellar is a public blockchain: every transaction is recorded onchain and visible to anyone' (privacy is NOT by default); Privacy Pools 'let users pay each other or interact with other protocols while optionally keeping their balances, transaction amounts, and addresses private'; 'Deposits and withdrawals into the pool are visible onchain, but transactions between parties within the pool do not need to be'; Confidential Tokens 'let users keep token balances and transaction amounts private while keeping the sender and receiver's addresses publicly visible onchain'; privacy is scoped to specific use cases (payments/settlement/payroll), with no general arbitrary-contract-call privacy described.

citations.source: Stellar blog — How Stellar Enables Private Payments with Zero-Knowledge Proofs / Stellar Private Payments coverage
citations.proves: Stellar frames privacy as opt-in zero-knowledge shielding of financial records ('individual financial records remain shielded'), not a fully private wallet or private-by-default model; the related Stellar Private Payments materials describe 'shielded deposits, transfers, and withdrawals,' confirming the 'shielded transfers' framing is Stellar's own.

citations.source: Stellar blog — Prototyping Privacy Pools on Stellar
citations.proves: Privacy Pools obscure the link between deposits and withdrawals while the deposits/withdrawals themselves remain visible, and use Association Set Providers for compliance — corroborating that only in-pool linkage is hidden, not the pool entry/exit, and that privacy is a discrete construct rather than a network-wide default.

crossChain.verdict: YES, you can showcase "USDC moves from Ethereum and arrives natively on Stellar" on real testnet today — Circle CCTP V2 is live on Stellar with documented testnet contracts and the standard sandbox Iris attestation API. That bridging leg is REAL, not a mock. The "then gets shielded" leg is the catch: it is NOT automatically atomic. Circle's Stellar CctpForwarder only does a plain SAC token TRANSFER to a recipient after minting — it does NOT call an arbitrary deposit() function on a destination contract. So mint-and-shield in one transaction only works if your privacy-pool contract is written to react to receiving USDC (a receiver-hook pattern), which is non-standard on Soroban. The honest, low-risk demo is TWO steps on the Stellar side: (1) CCTP mints native USDC to the user on Stellar testnet, then (2) a separate Soroban call deposits/shields it into the privacy pool. Both legs are real on testnet. The fully-atomic single-flow version is possible but is the riskiest unknown and probably more than a week of work because it depends on the privacy pool's contract interface, not on Circle.
crossChain.minimalDemo: Real end-to-end on TESTNET, two-leg version (recommended for ~1 week): (1) Get testnet USDC on Ethereum Sepolia from faucet.circle.com. (2) Source burn: approve USDC to TokenMessengerV2, then call TokenMessengerV2.depositForBurn on Sepolia targeting Stellar (destination domain = 27), with mintRecipient set to the Stellar CctpForwarder testnet contract (CA66Q2WFBND6V4UEB7RD4SAXSVIWMD6RA4X3U32ELVFGXV5PJK4T4VSZ) and hookData encoding the final Stellar recipient. (3) Poll the sandbox attestation API https://iris-api-sandbox.circle.com (v2 endpoint) until status=complete to get message+attestation bytes. (4) On Stellar testnet, invoke CctpForwarder.mint_and_forward(message, attestation) via Soroban RPC — native USDC is minted to the forwarder and transferred to your forward_recipient (a G/M/C strkey). This is the "arrives on Stellar" moment, fully real. (5) SHIELD as a second Soroban tx: call the privacy-pool transact() path with positive ext_amount using the just-minted USDC. The UI shows: Sepolia burn tx -> attestation polling -> Stellar mint tx -> shield tx. Every leg has a real testnet digest when the USDC pool leg is deployed and working.
integrationPath: Acquire Sepolia testnet USDC + ETH gas: faucet.circle.com (USDC for all listed testnets incl. Ethereum + Stellar) and any Sepolia ETH faucet.

Ethereum Sepolia burn: approve USDC then call TokenMessengerV2.depositForBurn (CCTP V2). Use Circle's published Ethereum Sepolia TokenMessengerV2/MessageTransmitterV2 addresses from developers.circle.com/cctp/references (EVM testnet). Set destinationDomain=27 (Stellar), mintRecipient=Stellar CctpForwarder, and hookData=forward_recipient strkey.
Attestation: poll https://iris-api-sandbox.circle.com v2 attestation endpoint by tx hash / message hash until the message is attested; retrieve message bytes + attestation signature.

Stellar testnet mint: build a Soroban tx invoking CctpForwarder.mint_and_forward(message, attestation) on contract CA66Q2WFBND6V4UEB7RD4SAXSVIWMD6RA4X3U32ELVFGXV5PJK4T4VSZ via @stellar/stellar-sdk + Soroban RPC; sign with a Stellar testnet keypair / Freighter.
Confirm native USDC landed (Stellar testnet USDC issuer/SAC); show balance + Stellar tx hash.

Shield leg (step 2, separate tx): call your Soroban privacy-pool transact() path with positive ext_amount using the minted USDC. For any atomic variant, custom adapter/pool work is required and must be verified first; this is the gating unknown.
Wire all four/five legs into the web app as a progress timeline, each step linking to a real explorer (Etherscan Sepolia / Stellar Expert testnet) — strong demo narrative.
crossChain.testnetStatus: REAL on testnet today. Circle CCTP V2 is deployed on Stellar with DOCUMENTED testnet contracts (developers.circle.com/cctp/references/stellar-contracts): TokenMessengerMinter CDNG7HXAPBWICI2E3AUBP3YZWZELJLYSB6F5CC7WLDTLTHVM74SLRTHP, MessageTransmitter CBJ6MTCKKZG73PMDZCJMSFRD7DQEMI4FKDH7CGDSV4W6FHCRBCQAVVJY, CctpForwarder CA66Q2WFBND6V4UEB7RD4SAXSVIWMD6RA4X3U32ELVFGXV5PJK4T4VSZ. Stellar CCTP domain = 27; Ethereum = domain 0 (Sepolia shares domain 0 and is explicitly listed as supported testnet). Sandbox attestation API = https://iris-api-sandbox.circle.com. The circlefin/stellar-cctp repo is V2 (deploy scripts are message-transmitter-v2 / token-messenger-minter-v2) and was last pushed 2026-06-18 (actively maintained). So Sepolia<->Stellar-testnet native USDC bridging is confirmed real. UNVERIFIED/needs live check: exact Ethereum Sepolia TokenMessengerV2 address (read from EVM testnet contracts page at build time), exact v2 Iris polling endpoint path, and whether Circle's faucet currently dispenses on BOTH Sepolia and Stellar testnet (it lists both).
risks: BIGGEST UNKNOWN: atomic mint-AND-shield is NOT free. Stellar CctpForwarder.mint_and_forward does a plain SAC token transfer to forward_recipient (confirmed in source: token_client.transfer(...)); it does NOT invoke a deposit/shield function. Atomic shielding requires the privacy-pool contract to implement a token-receiver hook and be set as forward_recipient — most Soroban contracts don't. Default to a 2-step demo (mint, then shield) to stay safe.

The 'privacy pool / shield' contract itself is the real scope risk, not the bridge. If you must write/deploy a Soroban shielded-deposit contract, that is the bulk of the week. Consider an existing testnet shielded-deposit contract or a clearly-labeled minimal shield to keep the bridge leg honest.

Cross-chain address encoding: EVM uses 32-byte addresses, Stellar uses strkey (G/M/C). mintRecipient and hookData must be correctly encoded as bytes or mint_and_forward reverts (InvalidMintRecipient / InvalidForwardRecipient / HookDataTooShort).
Attestation latency: CCTP requires source-chain finality before Circle attests; Sepolia confirmations + attestation can add minutes — build polling + a loading state, don't assume instant.
Exact EVM Sepolia V2 contract addresses and the precise v2 Iris endpoint path are unverified here; pull them from developers.circle.com at build time rather than hardcoding from memory.
Two SDK worlds: viem/ethers for Sepolia burn + @stellar/stellar-sdk Soroban for the mint. Soroban auth, signing (Freighter vs keypair), and fee bumping are a learning curve if the team is EVM-native.
dUSDC (your existing Predict token) is NOT USDC — this CCTP flow brings real Circle USDC to Stellar, a different asset/issuer; don't conflate them in the demo.

citations.proves: Stellar is a supported CCTP chain with domain ID 27; Ethereum is domain 0 and listed mainnets include their official testnets (e.g. Ethereum Sepolia).

Inbound minting on Stellar is done by calling mint_and_forward(message, attestation) on CctpForwarder; forward_recipient is a strkey (G/M/C, so a contract C-address is allowed) and the doc states the hook only encodes the recipient address, not a custom function call.

citations.source: Circle Docs — CCTP Stellar Contracts and Interfaces
citations.proves: Testnet contract IDs: TokenMessengerMinter CDNG7HXAPBWICI2E3AUBP3YZWZELJLYSB6F5CC7WLDTLTHVM74SLRTHP, MessageTransmitter CBJ6MTCKKZG73PMDZCJMSFRD7DQEMI4FKDH7CGDSV4W6FHCRBCQAVVJY, CctpForwarder CA66Q2WFBND6V4UEB7RD4SAXSVIWMD6RA4X3U32ELVFGXV5PJK4T4VSZ.

Standard flow = depositForBurn on source, Circle attestation, receiveMessage on destination; Iris attestation API testnet base URL = https://iris-api-sandbox.circle.com, mainnet = https://iris-api.circle.com; V2 endpoints under /v2/.

mint_and_forward, after minting via receive_message, executes token_client.transfer(&contract_address, &forward_recipient, &amount_minted) — a plain SAC transfer, NOT an arbitrary contract call; proving auto-shield is not atomic unless the recipient is a receiver-hook contract. Repo deploy scripts are -v2, confirming CCTP V2.
Official Circle Stellar CCTP repo, default branch master, pushed 2026-06-18 (actively maintained), Rust/Soroban; contains message-transmitter-v2 and token-messenger-minter-v2 deploy scripts.
Free testnet USDC is dispensed for supported chains including Ethereum and Stellar, enabling the source-side funding for a real Sepolia->Stellar demo without an operator-gated token.
